title: Small File Metadata Overhead in Object Storage
scenario: Your team is training a vision model on a cloud GPU cluster using a dataset of 10 million 50KB images stored individually in a cloud object storage bucket (e.g., S3). The training pipeline is heavily IO-bound. The EC2 instance has a 25 Gbps network interface, but the object storage has an average metadata latency (Time-To-First-Byte) of 20ms per GET request. Calculate the theoretical maximum throughput (images/sec) achievable by a single sequential data loader thread, and estimate the total time spent on metadata overhead for one full epoch compared to the actual data transfer time.
common_mistake: Calculating theoretical throughput based solely on the 25 Gbps network bandwidth (3.125 GB/s / 50KB = 62,500 images/sec), completely ignoring the per-file metadata latency which makes the pipeline IOPS-bound rather than bandwidth-bound.
realistic_solution: Consolidate the small image files into larger sequential formats (like TFRecords, Parquet, or WebDataset tar archives) sized around 100MB to 1GB each. This amortizes the 20ms metadata overhead across thousands of images, allowing the pipeline to utilize the full network bandwidth.
napkin_math: Single thread throughput = 1 request / (20ms TTFB + (50KB / 3.125GB/s transfer)) = 1 / ~0.020s = 50 images/sec. Total metadata time for 1 epoch (10M images) = 10,000,000 * 0.020s = 200,000 seconds (~55.5 hours). Actual data transfer time = (10,000,000 * 50KB) / 3.125GB/s = 500GB / 3.125GB/s = 160 seconds. Metadata overhead is >1200x the transfer time.

title: Diagnosing Sublinear Inference Scaling on H100
scenario: Your team migrated a 13B parameter LLM inference service (auto-regressive decoding, batch size 1) from NVIDIA A100-80GB to H100-80GB GPUs. The H100 boasts over 3x the FP16 dense compute of the A100 (989 TFLOPs vs 312 TFLOPs). However, profiling reveals the per-token generation latency only improved by roughly 1.6x. You are asked to analyze the system and root-cause why the application isn't achieving the expected >3x speedup.
common_mistake: Assuming the bottleneck is unoptimized CUDA kernels, CPU-to-GPU PCIe transfer overhead, or framework overhead, rather than recognizing the fundamental architectural limitation of the workload's arithmetic intensity.
realistic_solution: Identify that batch-1 LLM token generation is strictly memory-bandwidth bound because it must load the entire model weights from HBM to SRAM for every single token generated. The speedup is dictated entirely by the ratio of HBM bandwidths between the two GPUs, not their computational FLOPS. To escape the memory wall, increase arithmetic intensity via larger batch sizes (e.g., continuous batching).
napkin_math: A100-80GB HBM Bandwidth = 2.0 TB/s. H100-80GB HBM Bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s. Memory Bandwidth Ratio = 3.35 / 2.0 ≈ 1.67x. Expected speedup for strictly memory-bound workloads is ~1.67x, which perfectly matches the observed 1.6x improvement. The 3.1x compute ratio (989/312) is unreachable at batch size 1 where the arithmetic intensity is ~2 FLOPs/byte.

title: Diagnosing Small File Metadata Starvation
scenario: A deep learning team is training a vision model on a node with 8x A100 GPUs, loading a dataset of 10 million individual 100KB JPEG files from a network-attached parallel file system. The storage cluster advertises 20 GB/s of sequential read throughput and a metadata server limit of 10,000 file open operations per second. During training, the GPUs are starved, running at 15% utilization. Network monitoring shows storage throughput peaking at barely 1 GB/s, while CPU `iowait` is extremely high. Analyze the root cause of this I/O bottleneck.
common_mistake: Attempting to solve the issue by upgrading the storage network bandwidth or adding more parallel data loader workers, which actually exacerbates the metadata server bottleneck.
realistic_solution: Aggregate the individual JPEGs into large sequential container formats (like WebDataset, TFRecord, or Petastorm) to convert millions of metadata operations into a few large sequential reads, bypassing the IOPS bottleneck.
napkin_math: To feed 8x A100s, assume a target of 20,000 images/sec. At 1 file/image, this requires 20,000 file opens/sec. The DFS metadata server is capped at 10,000 opens/sec. The maximum achievable throughput is therefore 10,000 opens/sec * 100 KB = 1 GB/s, meaning the 20 GB/s bandwidth capacity is 95% unutilized due to metadata overhead.

title: Prometheus Cardinality Explosion
scenario: You are configuring Prometheus monitoring for a centralized LLM routing gateway serving multiple B2B clients across 5 global regions. You define a counter metric `llm_requests_total` with the following labels to track usage: `model_version` (10 active versions), `region` (5 regions), `gpu_type` (3 types), `http_status` (5 codes), and `customer_tenant_id` (10,000 enterprise tenants). Assuming each active time series consumes approximately 4 KB of RAM in the Prometheus TSDB, calculate the memory footprint of this single metric. How would you redesign the observability stack to support tenant-lev
common_mistake: Candidates often assume time series memory grows additively with new labels or rely on TSDB compression for active RAM, failing to recognize the multiplicative combinatorial explosion (cardinality) that high-entropy labels like `customer_tenant_id` cause.
realistic_solution: Remove the unbounded `customer_tenant_id` label from the Prometheus metric. High-cardinality data should be recorded via structured event logs (e.g., using FluentBit or OpenTelemetry) and sent to an OLAP database like ClickHouse or BigQuery for ad-hoc billing queries, leaving Prometheus to handle aggregate system health.
napkin_math: Cardinality = 10 (versions) × 5 (regions) × 3 (GPUs) × 5 (statuses) × 10,000 (tenants) = 7,500,000 unique time series. Memory Footprint = 7,500,000 × 4 KB = 30,000,000 KB = 30 GB of RAM for just ONE metric. Removing the tenant label drops cardinality to 750 series (3 MB).

title: Evaluating Storage Formats for Small-File Datasets
scenario: You are designing the data loading pipeline for a distributed training job processing 100 million 50KB image chips stored in a cloud network file system. The training cluster consists of 8x A100 GPUs, requiring an aggregate data feeding rate of 10,000 images per second (500 MB/s) to keep compute saturated. The data engineering team proposes storing the images as individual JPEG files in a deeply nested directory structure to allow for easy filtering and subset selection. Evaluate this proposal against the storage system's metadata overhead and justify your architectural decision.
common_mistake: Focusing solely on the raw sequential bandwidth capability of the storage system (e.g., 500 MB/s) while ignoring the IOPS limits and latency of metadata operations (like 'open' and 'close') required for millions of small files.
realistic_solution: Reject the individual file proposal. Consolidate the small images into larger, contiguous formats (e.g., TFRecords, Parquet, or WebDataset tarballs of 100MB-1GB). This amortizes the metadata lookup costs over thousands of samples and converts latency-bound random I/O into throughput-bound sequential reads.
napkin_math: With individual files: Assume a 2ms metadata latency to open a file over the network. Reading 50KB at 500MB/s takes 0.1ms. Total time per image = 2.1ms (max ~476 images/sec per thread). The metadata overhead is >95% of the read time. With consolidated files: Pack 2,000 images into a 100MB file. 1 open (2ms) + sequential read (200ms) = 202ms total for 2,000 images (~9,900 images/sec per thread). The metadata overhead drops to <1%.

title: Diagnosing Observability OOM Cascades
scenario: A Kubernetes cluster serving a transformer ranking model (10k QPS per pod on T4 GPUs) is experiencing cascade failures. The API pods are frequently evicted due to OOM errors, but GPU memory is stable at 14GB/16GB. The team recently updated the Prometheus `inference_latency_seconds` histogram to include labels for `model_version` (3 variants), `client_platform` (4 types), `country_code` (195), and `tenant_id` (10,000 unique B2B clients). Why are the pods running out of memory, and how do you fix it?
common_mistake: Focusing on the model's CPU memory footprint, dataloader memory leaks, or batch size, while ignoring the multiplicative explosion of time-series data generated by the monitoring sidecar.
realistic_solution: Identify that the high cardinality of the `tenant_id` label is causing Prometheus to create millions of unique time series in memory. Fix this by dropping `tenant_id` from the metric labels and moving high-cardinality tracking to structured logs or distributed trace exemplars.
napkin_math: Total unique time series = 3 (version) * 4 (platform) * 195 (country) * 10,000 (tenant) = 23,400,000. At ~10KB RAM per active time series in Prometheus, this requires 23.4M * 10KB = 234 GB of RAM, instantly exceeding standard pod memory limits.

title: Managing High Metric Cardinality in Global Model Serving
scenario: You are designing the observability stack for a recommendation model served across 5 global regions, processing 100K QPS. The team wants to track latency percentiles (P50, P90, P99) sliced by model_version (10 active), user_segment (1000 categories), device_type (50 types), and response_code (10 codes). The Prometheus-based TSDB is struggling with OOM crashes and high query latency. How do you evaluate the current metric design and what tradeoffs must you make to stabilize the observability platform while maintaining critical business visibility?
common_mistake: Suggesting simply scaling up the TSDB instances or adding more RAM without addressing the fundamental combinatorial explosion, or blindly dropping all high-cardinality labels without consulting product requirements.
realistic_solution: Shift high-cardinality dimensions (like user_segment) from metrics to structured logs or distributed tracing. Pre-aggregate critical dimensions at the collector level and limit TSDB labels to bounded global dimensions to keep active series manageable.
napkin_math: Current cardinality per metric = 5 (regions) * 10 (versions) * 1000 (segments) * 50 (devices) * 10 (codes) = 25,000,000 active time series. At ~3KB per series in RAM, this requires ~75GB for a single metric. Dropping user_segment reduces this to 25,000 series (~75MB).

title: ECMP Load Balancing for Mice Flows
scenario: A front-end API gateway handles incoming user traffic and generates 200,000 concurrent 'mice flows' per second. Each flow transmits a 15 KB payload to a pool of backend servers. The traffic is routed through a leaf switch that connects to four 40 Gbps uplinks using standard 5-tuple ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) hashing. Calculate the total aggregate bandwidth generated by these flows and determine the expected bandwidth utilization on each of the four uplinks.
common_mistake: Candidates often assume that a massive number of concurrent flows will overwhelm the switch's hashing tables or cause severe congestion via hash collisions, failing to recognize that ECMP thrives precisely on a large number of independent small flows due to statistical multiplexing.
realistic_solution: Calculate the total bandwidth (24 Gbps) and recognize that the Law of Large Numbers ensures 200,000 independent 5-tuples will distribute nearly perfectly across the 4 links, resulting in 6 Gbps per uplink.
napkin_math: Total Throughput = 200,000 flows/s * 15 KB/flow * 8 bits/byte = 24,000,000 Kbps = 24 Gbps. Expected load per link = 24 Gbps / 4 links = 6 Gbps. Utilization = 6 Gbps / 40 Gbps = 15% per link.

title: Evaluating Unified Network Fabrics for Mice and Elephant Flows
scenario: Your team is migrating a legacy web service cluster (handling 5 million requests/sec, average payload 5KB) into a shared multi-tenant 400Gbps Clos datacenter fabric that also hosts a 1,024-GPU distributed LLM training cluster. The current network uses static 5-tuple ECMP routing. The ML team complains that training iteration times vary by up to 40% depending on the web service load. Evaluate the architectural trade-offs between implementing flow-level Dynamic Load Balancing (DLB) versus transitioning to a cell-based, packet-spraying architecture to mitigate the impact of web service 'mice flow
common_mistake: Candidates often suggest prioritizing ML traffic via QoS/DSCP tags, failing to realize that QoS does not prevent the fundamental issue of hash polarization in static ECMP, where aggregate mice flows colliding on the same physical link will still reduce the bandwidth available to the elephant flow.
realistic_solution: Adopt a cell-based packet-spraying architecture (like Nvidia Spectrum-X or Broadcom Tomahawk 5). By breaking both mice and elephant flows into fixed-size cells (e.g., 256 bytes) and spraying them evenly across all available uplinks, the network achieves >90% link utilization and bypasses hash collisions, at the cost of requiring robust reordering buffers at the destination ToR.
napkin_math: Under ECMP with 4 uplinks (100Gbps each), if a 100Gbps elephant flow and 50Gbps of aggregated mice flows happen to hash to Link 1, the link is oversubscribed (150Gbps > 100Gbps), causing packet loss. With cell spraying, that same 150Gbps load is perfectly distributed at 37.5Gbps per link across all 4 links, operating safely below capacity.

title: Optimizing Arithmetic Intensity with Mini-Batching
scenario: You are training a dense neural network layer with an input dimension of 4096 and an output dimension of 4096 on an NVIDIA A100 GPU (memory bandwidth: 2039 GB/s, peak compute: 312 TFLOPs in FP16). The training loop currently processes one sample at a time (batch size = 1), resulting in severe underutilization. Calculate the required minimum mini-batch size to transition this layer from being memory-bound to compute-bound. Assume FP16 data types and ignore the memory bandwidth cost of activations for simplicity.
common_mistake: Focusing solely on FLOP utilization without analyzing the arithmetic intensity and memory bandwidth constraints of the matrix-vector multiplication.
realistic_solution: Calculate the A100's machine balance (FLOPs/Byte) and find the batch size where the arithmetic intensity of the matrix-matrix multiplication exceeds this threshold. A batch size of at least 154 (typically rounded to 256) is required.
napkin_math:
A100 Machine Balance = 312e12 FLOPs / 2039e9 Bytes/s ≈ 153 FLOPs/Byte.
For batch size B, Weight Memory (FP16) = 4096 * 4096 * 2 bytes = 33.55 MB.
FLOPs (Forward Pass) = 2 * 4096 * 4096 * B = 33.55M * B FLOPs.
Arithmetic Intensity ≈ (33.55M * B) / 33.55MB = B FLOPs/Byte.
To become compute-bound: Arithmetic Intensity > Machine Balance.
B > 153. A mini-batch size of 256 is recommended to fully utilize the hardware.

title: Diagnosing p99 Latency Spikes in Mixed-Flow Networks
scenario: A cloud-native ML inference cluster handles a mix of real-time user requests (mice flows, avg 2KB, 50k QPS) and background batch data ingestion (elephant flows, 5GB bursts). Both share 100 Gbps Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches with 16MB packet buffers. During data ingestion, average ToR link utilization peaks at just 40%, yet the p99 latency for real-time inference requests spikes from 20ms to over 500ms. Analyze the root cause of this p99 latency degradation despite the low overall bandwidth utilization.
common_mistake: Relying on average link utilization (40%) to rule out network congestion, and incorrectly attributing the latency spikes to application-level issues (e.g., CPU saturation or garbage collection) without checking switch buffer drops.
realistic_solution: Identify that bursty elephant flows cause microbursts that instantly saturate the 16MB ToR switch buffer, leading to tail drops for the latency-sensitive mice flows. Diagnose by monitoring switch buffer drop counters and microburst telemetry, rather than average interface utilization. Mitigate by implementing Quality of Service (QoS) queues to prioritize mice flows or using DCTCP to maintain shallow switch queues.
napkin_math: Buffer capacity at 100 Gbps = 16 MB / 100 Gbps = (16 * 8) Mb / 100,000 Mb/s = 1.28 ms of traffic. A sudden burst from the batch job fills this shallow buffer almost instantly. When a 2KB mice flow packet is dropped, the TCP Retransmission Timeout (RTO) is triggered (typically 200ms minimum in Linux). A single dropped packet instantly adds 200ms of latency, easily explaining the >500ms p99 spikes when compounded.

title: Estimating Mixed-Precision Memory Footprint
scenario: You are migrating a 10-billion parameter Transformer model to Mixed-Precision Training (AMP) using the Adam optimizer on a cluster of A100 GPUs. A junior engineer estimates that since FP16 uses half the bytes of FP32, the memory footprint for weights, gradients, and optimizer states will drop from 160 GB to 80 GB, theoretically allowing the model to fit on a single 80GB GPU with extreme activation checkpointing. Calculate the actual static memory footprint for these components under standard AMP.
common_mistake: Assuming that Mixed-Precision Training halves all memory requirements. Candidates often forget that AMP requires maintaining an FP32 master copy of the weights alongside the FP32 Adam moments (momentum and variance) to prevent underflow during gradient updates.
realistic_solution: The static memory footprint for weights, gradients, and optimizer states actually remains exactly the same at 16 bytes per parameter (160 GB total). It will still not fit on a single 80GB GPU. The true memory savings of AMP come primarily from storing forward activations in half-precision (FP16/BF16).
napkin_math:
Pure FP32 Training:
- Weights: 4B
- Gradients: 4B
- Adam (Momentum + Variance): 4B + 4B = 8B
Total = 16 Bytes/param.

Mixed-Precision (AMP):
- FP16 Weights: 2B
- FP16 Gradients: 2B
- FP32 Master Weights: 4B
- FP32 Adam States: 8B
Total = 16 Bytes/param.

Total for 10B parameters = 10 * 10^9 * 16 bytes ≈ 160 GB in both configurations.

title: Diagnosing Mixed-Precision OOM Failures
scenario: A machine learning team transitions a 7B parameter causal language model from standard FP32 training to FP16 Mixed-Precision training on a single node of 8x 80GB A100 GPUs, aiming to increase their batch size. They use PyTorch DDP and the AdamW optimizer. To their surprise, immediately after initializing the model and optimizer (before the first forward pass), the script crashes with a CUDA Out of Memory (OOM) error. Diagnose why the static memory footprint increased when moving to 'reduced' precision.
common_mistake: Candidates often assume mixed-precision halves all memory requirements (from 4 bytes to 2 bytes per parameter), failing to account for the FP32 master weights and FP32 optimizer states required to prevent gradient update underflow.
realistic_solution: Identify that standard mixed-precision actually increases the parameter memory footprint from 16 bytes/param to 20 bytes/param. To resolve the OOM on 80GB GPUs, the team must shard the optimizer states across the 8 GPUs using ZeRO Stage 1 or PyTorch FSDP.
napkin_math: FP32 Training: (4B weights + 4B grads + 8B Adam states) * 7B params = 112 GB total. Mixed-Precision Training: (2B FP16 weights + 2B FP16 grads + 4B FP32 master weights + 8B FP32 Adam states) * 7B params = 140 GB total. Memory footprint increased by 28 GB.

title: Diagnosing A100 Underutilization in NLP Training
scenario: A team is training a 350M parameter transformer model on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU (19.5 TFLOPs FP32 compute, 1.5 TB/s memory bandwidth). They are processing sequences of length 512 with a micro-batch size of 2. Despite having plenty of free VRAM (well under the 40GB limit), they observe low GPU utilization (~30%) and poor training throughput. They suspect a PyTorch dataloader bottleneck, but profiling shows data loading is instantaneous. How would you analyze the root cause of this underutilization?
common_mistake: Focusing solely on dataloader issues, CPU bottlenecks, or suggesting gradient accumulation without analyzing the arithmetic intensity of the operations at small batch sizes.
realistic_solution: Analyze the arithmetic intensity. A batch size of 2 results in operations that are heavily memory-bandwidth bound. The GPU spends most of its time loading weight matrices from HBM rather than performing calculations. By increasing the mini-batch size (e.g., to 32 or 64) until VRAM is fully utilized, the workload transforms from memory-bound vector operations to compute-bound matrix multiplications, significantly improving hardware utilization.
napkin_math: A100 Ridge Point = 19.5 TFLOPs / 1.5 TB/s ≈ 13 FLOPs/byte. For a linear layer (y=xW), loading weights W takes 4 bytes/parameter (FP32). FLOPs = 2 * Batch * Params. Arithmetic Intensity (AI) ≈ (2 * B * Params) / (4 * Params) = B/2 FLOPs/byte. If B=2, AI = 1 FLOP/byte (highly memory bound). To reach the ridge point of 13, we need B/2 ≥ 13, so a batch size of at least 26 is required to become compute bound.

title: Evaluating FP16 vs BF16 for LLM Pre-training Stability
scenario: Your team is pre-training a 30B parameter LLM on a cluster of 512 NVIDIA A100 GPUs (80GB). Using standard FP16 mixed precision with dynamic loss scaling, the training frequently crashes with NaNs due to gradient overflow, leading to wasted compute. You are tasked with evaluating whether to switch to BF16 mixed precision or stick with FP16 and tune the loss scaler. Evaluate the memory bandwidth, compute throughput (both formats use 16-bit Tensor Cores on A100 delivering 312 TFLOPS), and numerical stability trade-offs to justify your architectural decision.
common_mistake: Assuming BF16 is a strictly superior drop-in replacement without realizing that its 7-bit mantissa requires strictly maintaining FP32 master weights to avoid losing small gradient updates during the optimizer step.
realistic_solution: Switch to BF16 for the forward and backward passes to eliminate gradient overflow, while maintaining FP32 master weights for the optimizer step. Since A100 Tensor Cores process both FP16 and BF16 at 312 TFLOPS and both consume 2 bytes per parameter, there is zero performance or memory bandwidth penalty for switching to BF16, while its 8-bit exponent entirely removes the need for dynamic loss scaling.
napkin_math: FP16 max value = 65,504 (often exceeded in LLM training). BF16 max value = 3.4e38 (matches FP32). Performance: FP16 TFLOPS = BF16 TFLOPS = 312 on A100. Memory bandwidth: 2 bytes per parameter for both. However, BF16 machine epsilon is ~7.8e-3. An update of 1e-4 to a weight of 1.0 yields 1.0 in BF16 (1.0 + 1e-4 < 1.0 + epsilon), proving the necessity of FP32 master weights for accumulation.

title: Evaluating Mini-Batch Size on A100
scenario: You are designing a training pipeline for a large MLP on an NVIDIA A100 GPU (312 TFLOPs FP16, 1.5 TB/s HBM bandwidth). The data science team insists on using a batch size of 16 to maximize the number of gradient updates per epoch and improve statistical convergence. Evaluate this architectural decision from a systems perspective, detailing the hardware implications, and justify a mathematically optimal alternative batch size.
common_mistake: Focusing solely on statistical efficiency (convergence per epoch) while ignoring hardware arithmetic intensity, failing to realize that small batches leave the GPU starved for data and vastly underutilized.
realistic_solution: Increase the batch size to at least 256 (or 512) to shift the operation from memory-bound to compute-bound. This converts vector-matrix operations into matrix-matrix multiplications, raising the arithmetic intensity above the hardware's ridge point.
napkin_math: A100 ridge point = 312 TFLOPs / 1.5 TB/s = 208 FLOPs/byte. For a large MLP layer (XW), Arithmetic Intensity (AI) ≈ Batch Size (B). At B=16, AI ≈ 16 FLOPs/byte < 208 (severely memory bound). Max compute = 16 FLOPs/byte * 1.5 TB/s = 24 TFLOPs (8% utilization). Increasing to B=256 makes AI ≈ 256 FLOPs/byte > 208, allowing the GPU to achieve compute-bound peak performance.

title: MoE Active Parameter Bandwidth
scenario: You are deploying a 160B parameter Mixture of Experts (MoE) LLM on a node with 8x A100 80GB GPUs. The architecture consists of 40B shared parameters (attention and embeddings) and 8 experts sharing the remaining 120B parameters equally. The router selects the top-2 experts per token. Assuming FP16 precision (2 bytes/parameter) and a batch size of 1, calculate both the total GPU memory required to host the model weights and the memory bandwidth consumed to generate a single token during decoding.
common_mistake: Assuming memory bandwidth per token requires reading all 160B parameters (320GB) like a dense model, rather than calculating based only on the active parameter count.
realistic_solution: Calculate the memory footprint for weights using the total parameter count, but calculate the decoding bandwidth using only the active parameter count. The active parameters per token are the 40B shared parameters plus 2 selected experts at 15B each (120B / 8 experts), totaling 70B active parameters. At FP16, generating one token requires reading 140GB of weights.
napkin_math:
Total Weight Memory = 160B params * 2 bytes = 320GB.
Expert Size = 120B / 8 = 15B params.
Active Params = 40B (shared) + 2 * 15B (experts) = 70B params.
Bandwidth per token (BS=1) = 70B * 2 bytes = 140GB.

title: Diagnosing MoE Routing and OOM Bottlenecks
scenario: You are serving an 8x7B Mixture of Experts (MoE) model (56B total parameters, 14B active per token) across an 8x A100 80GB node using Expert Parallelism (1 expert per GPU). The total model weights are ~112GB in FP16, easily fitting in the 640GB cluster memory. However, at a batch size of 256 and sequence length of 2048, you experience intermittent Out-of-Memory (OOM) crashes on specific GPUs and massive tail latency spikes. Standard metrics show average cluster GPU utilization is below 40%. Diagnose the root cause of the OOM crashes and the latency degradation.
common_mistake: Candidates often blame the total KV cache size or general all-to-all communication overhead, missing that Expert Parallelism makes individual GPU memory and compute dependent on dynamic routing distributions. They often suggest global batch size reduction instead of addressing expert imbalance.
realistic_solution: The root cause is severe load imbalance (token skew) where the router sends a disproportionate number of tokens to a single expert. This causes the target GPU to exceed its 80GB VRAM limit due to localized activation and KV cache spikes, and serializes compute, causing tail latency. The fix is to implement a strict expert capacity limit (token dropping/overflow to other experts) and combine Expert Parallelism with Tensor Parallelism to distribute the hot expert's memory footprint.
napkin_math: Total VRAM: 8x80GB = 640GB. Model: 56B * 2B = 112GB (14GB/GPU). Remaining VRAM/GPU: 66GB. Normal per-GPU KV/activations at balanced load (1/8th of tokens): ~8GB. If routing is heavily skewed (e.g., 90% of tokens to Expert 2), GPU 2 processes ~470k tokens instead of 65k. The activation/KV memory for 470k tokens on a single GPU balloons past the 66GB remaining headroom, causing an OOM on GPU 2 while GPUs 0, 1, 3-7 sit idle with plenty of free memory.

title: Evaluating MoE Serving Topologies on H100 Nodes
scenario: Your team is deploying an 8x22B Mixture of Experts (MoE) LLM (141B total parameters, 39B active per token) on a node with 8xH100 80GB GPUs connected via NVSwitch. Serving with standard Tensor Parallelism (TP=8) yields poor throughput due to massive communication bottlenecks during expert routing. You must evaluate alternative parallelization strategies (Expert Parallelism vs. TP vs. Pipeline Parallelism) to justify the optimal topology for maximizing batch size and throughput while remaining within the 640GB total VRAM limit.
common_mistake: Treating the MoE model exactly like a dense model by defaulting to TP=8 across all layers, ignoring the severe All-to-All communication overhead introduced by token routing in the MoE layers.
realistic_solution: Implement a hybrid strategy using Tensor Parallelism for the dense attention layers and Expert Parallelism (EP=8) for the MoE layers. By placing one expert on each GPU, you isolate the massive parameters while utilizing the 900 GB/s NVSwitch efficiently for All-to-All token routing, allowing you to maximize the KV cache size.
napkin_math: Total model size at FP16 = 141B * 2B = 282GB. Node VRAM = 8 * 80GB = 640GB. Remaining VRAM for KV cache = 640GB - 282GB = 358GB. With EP=8, each GPU holds ~35GB of weights. The All-to-All routing communication moves ~O(batch_size * sequence_length * hidden_dim) bytes. For batch 256, seq 2048, hidden 4096: ~2GB transferred. Over a 900 GB/s NVSwitch, this takes <3ms per layer, which is trivial compared to the compute gains.

title: Architecting a Multi-Tier Storage Hierarchy for H100 Clusters
scenario: You are tasked with designing the data loading architecture for a large-scale training cluster of 256 nodes (each with 8x H100 GPUs). The training dataset is 5 PB of high-resolution images residing in an AWS S3 object store. The GPUs require a combined 250 GB/s of training data throughput to remain fully utilized. Fetching directly from the object store currently yields only 50 GB/s, resulting in severe GPU starvation. Evaluate the trade-offs between implementing a centralized all-flash parallel file system versus a decentralized caching tier using local NVMe SSDs on the compute nodes, and jus
common_mistake: Candidates often reflexively recommend a massive centralized parallel file system (like Lustre or Weka) to hit the 250 GB/s requirement, ignoring the immense network cross-sectional bandwidth required and the prohibitive cost of caching 5 PB in an all-flash NAS.
realistic_solution: The optimal design is a decentralized multi-tier storage hierarchy. Use the object store as the cheap, high-capacity source of truth. Equip each of the 256 compute nodes with local NVMe Gen4 SSDs to act as an ephemeral caching tier. An orchestrator (like Ray Data) asynchronously pre-fetches the upcoming batches from S3 into the local NVMes. The GPUs then read directly from local NVMe, providing massive aggregate throughput without saturating the cluster's network fabric.
napkin_math:
Total throughput needed = 250 GB/s. Per node throughput = 250 / 256 ≈ 1 GB/s. 
Local NVMe array (e.g., 4x Gen4 drives) provides ~25 GB/s read bandwidth per node. 
Reading 1 GB/s uses <4% of the node's local disk bandwidth, leaving PCIe lanes free for GPU transfers. 
Meanwhile, pre-fetching from S3 only requires 1 GB/s (8 Gbps) per node over the network, completely bypassing the need for a multi-million dollar 2 Tbps centralized all-flash array.

title: Diagnosing NVMe Cache Thrashing in CV Training
scenario: A team is training a distributed Computer Vision model on 256 A100 GPUs across 32 nodes. The 150TB dataset is stored in cloud object storage. To fix an I/O bottleneck (25% GPU utilization), they add 4TB of local NVMe storage to each node to act as an LRU cache. However, GPU utilization only rises to 30%, and `iostat` shows continuous 100% NVMe write utilization. The training framework uses a true global random shuffle across the entire dataset every epoch.
common_mistake: Assuming that simply inserting a high-speed NVMe tier into the storage hierarchy automatically mitigates object storage latency, without accounting for how global random access patterns interact with cache eviction.
realistic_solution: The global random shuffle causes severe cache thrashing. The team should implement deterministic node-level sharding (assigning a fixed subset of data to each node per epoch) and shuffle locally, or adopt a sequential streaming format like WebDataset to bypass local caching for single-epoch random reads.
napkin_math: Total dataset = 150TB. A single node's cache = 4TB. With a uniform global random shuffle, the probability of a node needing a sample that is already in its cache is roughly 4TB / 150TB ≈ 2.6%. This means ~97.4% of data requests result in a cache miss, forcing a fetch from object storage and a subsequent write/eviction on the NVMe drive, ultimately bottlenecking the node on disk writes.

title: Multi-Tier Pre-fetch Sizing for 3D ViT
scenario: You are training a 3D Vision Transformer on a node with 8x H100 GPUs. Each GPU processes 10 batches per second, with each batch containing 32 high-resolution medical images totaling 200MB per batch. Your 50TB dataset resides in S3, which has a 5 GB/s throughput limit to your compute node. The node has a local NVMe RAID array capable of 25 GB/s read throughput. Calculate the total data ingestion rate of the node, determine if direct S3 streaming is viable, and calculate how long it takes to pre-fetch a 2TB epoch partition from S3 to the local NVMe cache.
common_mistake: Candidates often assume cloud object storage throughput is sufficient for direct streaming without calculating the aggregate multi-GPU ingestion rate, leading to severe I/O starvation.
realistic_solution: First calculate the node ingestion rate (16 GB/s). Recognize this exceeds the 5 GB/s S3 network limit, meaning direct streaming will starve GPUs. The solution is to use the local NVMe as an intermediate caching tier, pre-fetching the 2TB epoch which takes 400 seconds over the network.
napkin_math: Node Ingestion = 8 GPUs * 10 batches/s * 200 MB/batch = 16,000 MB/s = 16 GB/s. Since 16 GB/s > 5 GB/s (S3 throughput), NVMe caching is mandatory. Pre-fetch time = 2,000 GB / 5 GB/s = 400 seconds.

title: Production-Weighted Model Quality Benchmarking
scenario: You are benchmarking an INT4-quantized 8B parameter model for a cloud-based coding assistant. The baseline FP16 model achieves 88.5% overall production accuracy. Your production workload distribution is 70% short-context completions (where INT4 accuracy slightly drops from 90% to 88%) and 30% long-context RAG queries (where INT4 accuracy drops significantly from 85% to 60% due to unhandled activation outliers). Standard public benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval) missed this regression because they heavily skew toward short contexts. Calculate the expected overall production accuracy of the INT4 model
common_mistake: Candidates often rely solely on aggregate public benchmark scores without weighting the accuracy by the actual production request distribution, missing severe regressions in critical sub-populations like long-context generation.
realistic_solution: Calculate the production-weighted accuracy to reveal the true degradation. The INT4 model's expected overall accuracy drops by 8.9%. To keep the overall degradation under 2%, the long-context RAG accuracy must be improved to at least 83% (e.g., by using outlier-aware quantization techniques like AWQ or SmoothQuant).

title: MLPerf Time-to-Train Cost Estimation
scenario: You are evaluating cloud instances for training a BERT-Large style model on a proprietary 300GB corpus, which is exactly 10x the size of the standard MLPerf reference dataset. Vendor A offers an 8x A100 node at $32/hour with a published MLPerf Training time-to-train (TTT) of 18 minutes. Vendor B offers an 8x H100 node at $90/hour with an MLPerf TTT of 5.5 minutes. Assuming linear scaling with dataset size to reach the same target quality, calculate the estimated training time and total cost for both options to select the most cost-effective instance.
common_mistake: Looking solely at the 2.8x higher hourly rate of the H100 node and rejecting it as too expensive without factoring in the absolute Time-to-Train (TTT) reduction.
realistic_solution: Scale the baseline MLPerf TTT by the 10x dataset multiplier to find the total expected training time, then multiply by the hourly rate. The H100 completes the workload fast enough to offset its premium hourly price, making it cheaper overall.
napkin_math:
A100 Node: 18 min * 10 = 180 mins (3.0 hours). Total Cost = 3.0 hrs * $32/hr = $96.00.
H100 Node: 5.5 min * 10 = 55 mins (~0.916 hours). Total Cost = 0.916 hrs * $90/hr = $82.50.
The H100 node is cheaper by $13.50 per training run.

title: Evaluating FP8 Trade-offs in MLPerf Time-to-Train
scenario: You are preparing an official MLPerf Training v4.0 submission for an LLM on a 1024x H100 cluster. The baseline BF16 training achieves a step time of 2.0s and converges to the MLPerf target quality (perplexity) in exactly 50,000 steps. The hardware team proposes switching to FP8 precision. FP8 reduces the step time to 1.2s due to doubled Tensor Core throughput and reduced memory bandwidth pressure. However, the reduced precision introduces statistical inefficiency; the model now requires learning rate adjustments and requires 70,000 steps to reach the identical target perplexity. Evaluate wheth
common_mistake: Candidates often focus solely on the raw hardware throughput increase (Tokens/sec or MFU) and assume step-time reduction linearly translates to benchmark victory, ignoring that MLPerf strictly measures 'Time to Train' to a specific convergence target, penalizing statistical inefficiency.
realistic_solution: Calculate the absolute Time-to-Train (TTT) for both configurations. The MLPerf primary metric is wall-clock time to target quality, not raw throughput. By multiplying the step time by the required steps to converge, we determine the actual TTT. Despite requiring 40% more steps due to FP8's statistical degradation, the 40% reduction in step time outpaces the convergence penalty, resulting in a lower overall wall-clock time. FP8 should be adopted.
napkin_math: BF16 TTT = 2.0s/step * 50,000 steps = 100,000 seconds (~27.7 hours). FP8 TTT = 1.2s/step * 70,000 steps = 84,000 seconds (~23.3 hours). Absolute time saved = 16,000 seconds. Overall MLPerf speedup = 100,000 / 84,000 = 1.19x.

title: Diagnosing Activation Outliers in INT8 Quantized LLMs
scenario: Your team quantized an 8B parameter LLM from FP16 to INT8 (W8A8) for serving on Nvidia L4 GPUs, aiming for a 2x throughput gain. Automated benchmarking shows only a 0.5% drop in aggregate MMLU score (from 68.4% to 67.9%). However, after canary deployment, production monitoring reveals that 4% of customer support queries involving context longer than 2,048 tokens result in complete gibberish or severe hallucination. Analyze the discrepancy between the benchmarking results and production failures, and identify the root cause.
common_mistake: Assuming aggregate multiple-choice benchmarks like MMLU adequately cover the full input distribution, and failing to recognize that large activation outliers emerge primarily in long-context sequences, which causes destructive clipping in naive INT8 quantization.
realistic_solution: Diagnose that standard W8A8 quantization clips emergent activation outliers specific to long sequences. Implement sequence-length stratified benchmarking to catch this, and switch to techniques like SmoothQuant to mathematically migrate quantization difficulty from activations to weights.
napkin_math: If typical activations are in [-5, 5], the INT8 scale factor is 5/127 = 0.039. A long-context outlier of magnitude 150 gets clipped to 5 (an absolute error of 145). Across just 10 outlier channels in a 4096-dim hidden state, this introduces massive distortion into the downstream attention softmax, collapsing generation quality.

title: Model Card Disaggregated Performance SLA
scenario: You are creating a Model Card for a cloud-based clinical triage NLP model deployed on AWS Inferentia2 instances. The model reports an impressive 92% overall accuracy on a 100,000-patient test set. The hospital's SLA mandates a minimum 85% accuracy across all demographic subgroups to ensure equitable care. The test set comprises three demographics: Group A (80,000 patients, 94% accuracy), Group B (15,000 patients, 86% accuracy), and Group C (5,000 patients). Calculate the disaggregated accuracy for Group C to complete the model card's quantitative analysis section and determine if the deploymen
common_mistake: Reporting only the aggregate 92% accuracy in the model card and approving the deployment, falsely assuming the high overall score guarantees all minority subgroups meet the 85% SLA threshold.
realistic_solution: Calculate the exact accuracy for Group C, which reveals a hidden performance degradation (78%). This violates the SLA, meaning the model card must document this limitation and deployment must be blocked pending targeted retraining.
napkin_math:
Total correct predictions = 0.92 * 100,000 = 92,000
Group A correct = 0.94 * 80,000 = 75,200
Group B correct = 0.86 * 15,000 = 12,900
Group C correct = 92,000 - (75,200 + 12,900) = 3,900
Group C Accuracy = 3,900 / 5,000 = 78%
78% < 85% SLA threshold (Deployment fails)

title: Large Batch MLPerf Convergence Analysis
scenario: Your team is running the MLPerf Training v3.0 benchmark for GPT-3 (175B) on 512 H100 GPUs. To improve hardware utilization, you double the global batch size from 2M to 4M tokens, which successfully increases overall cluster throughput by 25% (tokens/sec). However, your official MLPerf 'Time to Train' metric regresses by 20%, failing to meet the target baseline. Individual step times are deterministic and there are no network bottlenecks. Analyze why the MLPerf metric regressed despite the increased hardware throughput.
common_mistake: Assuming that higher cluster throughput (tokens/sec or TFLOPS) directly translates to a faster MLPerf 'Time to Train', leading candidates to falsely diagnose non-existent network stragglers or CPU bottlenecks.
realistic_solution: The regression is caused by exceeding the model's critical batch size. MLPerf measures strictly wall-clock time-to-convergence to a target validation metric, not raw throughput. While the larger batch size processed tokens faster, the statistical efficiency per token plummeted. The model required significantly more total tokens (and thus steps) to converge. The solution is to retune the learning rate schedule (e.g., applying the linear scaling rule with warmup) or revert to the optimal batch size.
napkin_math:
Baseline: B=2M, 1.0s/step, 1000 steps to target loss -> T_train = 1000s. Throughput = 2M/1.0 = 2M tok/s.
New: B=4M, 1.6s/step. Throughput = 4M/1.6 = 2.5M tok/s (25% higher throughput).
Due to statistical efficiency loss, convergence requires 750 steps (instead of an ideal 500).
New T_train = 750 * 1.6s = 1200s (20% regression in time-to-train).

title: Root-Causing Deployment Failures via Disaggregated Evaluation
scenario: A cloud-hosted NLP moderation API processing 2,000 requests/sec on a cluster of g5.12xlarge instances sees its overall accuracy plummet from 92% to 70% following a global regional rollout. The engineering team assumes concept drift, spends $15,000 retraining the model on a naive random sample of the new data, but only improves aggregate accuracy to 74%. The upstream vendor provided a Model Card that only reported aggregate F1 scores and a generic 'intended use' statement. How do you root-cause this systemic failure, and what specific statistical artifacts were missing from the Model Card?
common_mistake: Candidates often blame infrastructure bottlenecks or suggest blind hyperparameter tuning, completely missing that the model is operating outside its intended domain and lacking visibility into subpopulation performance.
realistic_solution: Root-cause the failure by computing disaggregated evaluation metrics across regional slices. The Model Card failed to document the severe performance degradation on non-majority geographic slices, which were hidden by the original heavily skewed validation set. Mandate stratified reporting and explicit out-of-scope use cases in the documentation.
napkin_math: Original skewed validation: 95% Region A (94% acc), 5% Region B (54% acc) -> Aggregate = (0.95 * 0.94) + (0.05 * 0.54) = 92%. New global traffic mix: 40% Region A, 60% Region B -> Expected Aggregate = (0.40 * 0.94) + (0.60 * 0.54) = 37.6% + 32.4% = 70%. The deployment failure was mathematically predictable if the Model Card contained the disaggregated 54% subpopulation metric.

title: Benchmarking Quantization Degradation in Generative LLMs
scenario: You are evaluating a new INT8 SmoothQuant strategy for a production 70B LLM deployed on an 8x A100 80GB node. The offline benchmark suite (MMLU, TruthfulQA) shows a negligible 0.5% accuracy drop compared to the FP16 baseline. However, the initial canary deployment for a code-completion task results in a 15% increase in syntax errors and a 30% drop in user acceptance rate. Evaluate the trade-offs of the current benchmarking approach, diagnose why the offline metrics failed to predict production degradation, and design a rigorous benchmarking strategy to validate model quality under quantization
common_mistake: Assuming static, multiple-choice benchmarks like MMLU adequately measure the generative quality of a quantized LLM, failing to recognize that quantization errors compound autoregressively during long-context generation.
realistic_solution: Transition from static multiple-choice tests to execution-based evaluations (e.g., HumanEval) and generative benchmarks (e.g., MT-Bench). Additionally, measure the KL-divergence of the output logits between the FP16 teacher and INT8 model on a representative sample of production prompts to detect long-tail degradation.
napkin_math: Let baseline per-token error rate be e_fp16 = 0.001 and e_int8 = 0.005. For a 1-token MMLU answer, INT8 success is (1-0.005) = 99.5%. For a 200-token code snippet, sequence success is P = (1 - e)^200. FP16: 0.999^200 ≈ 81.8%. INT8: 0.995^200 ≈ 36.7%. A small per-token error increase causes an exponential drop in long-sequence accuracy.

title: 40B Model Cold-Start Latency Calculation
scenario: You are provisioning a new inference node with a single PCIe H100 GPU (PCIe Gen5 x16, 64 GB/s) to serve a 40B parameter model in FP16. The node must fetch the model checkpoint from a remote object store over a 100 Gbps network interface during cold-start. Assuming perfectly pipelined downloading and host-to-device transfers, calculate the minimum theoretical cold-start latency before the model is fully resident in HBM.
common_mistake: Candidates often use the HBM bandwidth or PCIe bandwidth to calculate the loading time, ignoring the network interface bottleneck. Another common error is failing to divide by 8 to convert 100 Gbps to 12.5 GB/s.
realistic_solution: Identify the slowest component in the data path (the network). Convert the 40B FP16 model to its byte size (80 GB), convert network speed to GB/s (12.5 GB/s), and calculate the latency based on the network bottleneck.
napkin_math:
1. Model Size = 40B parameters * 2 bytes/param (FP16) = 80 GB.
2. Network Bandwidth = 100 Gbps / 8 = 12.5 GB/s.
3. PCIe Gen5 Bandwidth = 64 GB/s (faster than network, not the bottleneck).
4. Cold-Start Time = 80 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 6.4 seconds.

title: Diagnosing LLM Cold-Start Loading Bottlenecks
scenario: You are investigating a severe cold-start latency regression when auto-scaling a 140GB LLM (FP16) on a node with 8x A100 80GB GPUs. The model weights are pulled from cloud object storage via a 10 Gbps network link, staged to a local NVMe SSD (3 GB/s sequential read), and loaded into HBM. Auto-scaling logs show a 4.5-minute delay before the container starts accepting requests. Diagnose the primary structural bottlenecks contributing to this delay and identify the specific steps in the loading pipeline causing the overhead.
common_mistake: Candidates often blame the PCIe bandwidth or HBM transfer rates, failing to analyze the end-to-end pipeline. They frequently miss the massive overhead of standard PyTorch pickle deserialization on the CPU, which adds minutes to the load time.
realistic_solution: Root cause analysis reveals three bottlenecks: the 10 Gbps network link, sequential disk I/O, and CPU-bound deserialization. The solution is to switch to SafeTensors for zero-copy memory mapping (`mmap`), bypassing CPU unpickling overhead, and utilizing host-level caching (or higher network bandwidth) to eliminate the object storage pull.
napkin_math: 1. Network pull: 140 GB / (10 Gbps / 8 bits) = 112 seconds. 2. Disk Read: 140 GB / 3 GB/s = ~47 seconds. 3. CPU Deserialization (Pickle): ~100-150 seconds (single-threaded bottleneck). 4. PCIe Gen4 x16 transfer: 140 GB / 32 GB/s = ~4.5 seconds. Total = 112s + 47s + 120s + 4.5s = ~283 seconds (4.7 minutes). The network and CPU deserialization are the dominant factors.

title: Trade-offs in Disaggregated Performance vs Latency
scenario: You are evaluating two LLMs for a medical triage application. The 13B quantized model (running on 1x A100 40GB) achieves an average 150ms TTFT and costs $1.50/hr. Its Model Card shows an F1 score of 0.94 for the majority demographic (80% of users) but 0.78 for a minority demographic (20% of users). The 70B model (running on 4x A100 80GB) costs $12.00/hr, achieves 350ms TTFT, and has a uniform F1 of 0.92. The strict system SLA requires <200ms average TTFT. Evaluate the trade-offs and propose an architecture that satisfies latency, cost, and fairness constraints.
common_mistake: Only looking at the aggregate F1 score (0.908 for 13B) and deploying the cheaper model, or unconditionally deploying the 70B model which violates the 200ms SLA and explodes operational costs.
realistic_solution: Implement a hybrid routing architecture. Use a lightweight classifier to route queries affecting the minority demographic to the 70B model, while the 13B model serves the remaining 80%. This satisfies fairness criteria while maintaining the overall SLA.
napkin_math: Average TTFT = (0.8 * 150ms) + (0.2 * 350ms) = 190ms (Meets <200ms SLA). Effective Hourly Cost = (0.8 * $1.50) + (0.2 * $12.00) = $1.20 + $2.40 = $3.60/hr (70% savings vs exclusive 70B deployment). Minimum F1 is now 0.92 across all users.

title: Evaluating Storage Backends for LLM Cold-Starts
scenario: You are designing the autoscaling architecture for a 175B parameter LLM (350GB in FP16) deployed on an 8x H100 instance. To handle bursty traffic, new instances must be provisioned quickly. You are evaluating two options for loading the model weights into HBM: 1) Streaming directly from a regional Object Store over a 100 Gbps network interface, or 2) Pre-loading weights onto a local PCIe Gen4 NVMe SSD capable of 12 GB/s sequential read. Evaluate the cold-start latency tradeoffs between these two storage backends and justify which architecture you would choose to meet a strict 45-second weight-
common_mistake: Candidates often instinctively assume local NVMe SSDs are strictly faster than network storage, failing to convert Gbps to GB/s and realizing that a 100 Gbps network link (12.5 GB/s) can rival or exceed the sequential read throughput of a single high-end local NVMe drive.
realistic_solution: Choose the Object Store streaming approach. At 100 Gbps (12.5 GB/s), loading 350GB takes ~28 seconds. The local NVMe at 12 GB/s takes ~29 seconds. Both comfortably meet the 45-second SLA, but streaming directly from the object store eliminates the massive operational overhead and cost of pre-baking or replicating 350GB of weights to the local disk of every ephemeral autoscaling node.
napkin_math: Object Store: 100 Gbps = 12.5 GB/s. Load time = 350 GB / 12.5 GB/s = 28.0 seconds. Local NVMe: 12 GB/s. Load time = 350 GB / 12 GB/s = 29.2 seconds.

title: Dynamic Ensemble Compute vs Memory
scenario: A cloud fraud detection service uses an ensemble of 4 specialized models to handle distribution shifts in transaction patterns across different geographical regions. Each specialized expert model requires 20 TFLOPS per inference and 2GB of VRAM. A gating network determines the routing and requires 1 TFLOP and 0.5GB of VRAM. Calculate the per-request compute (TFLOPS) and total active GPU memory (VRAM) footprint required for 'soft routing' (computing and weighting all 4 outputs) versus 'hard routing' (dynamically selecting and executing only the top-1 expert).
common_mistake: Assuming that hard routing reduces the memory footprint proportionally to the compute reduction, forgetting that all specialized models must remain permanently loaded in VRAM to meet strict latency SLAs and avoid PCIe transfer bottlenecks.
realistic_solution: Hard routing reduces the compute requirement by 74% (down to 21 TFLOPS) but requires the exact same 8.5GB of VRAM as soft routing, as dynamically loading models from host memory per-request would violate real-time latency constraints.
napkin_math: Soft Routing Compute: 1 TFLOP (gating) + (4 * 20 TFLOPS) = 81 TFLOPS. Soft Routing VRAM: 0.5GB + (4 * 2GB) = 8.5GB. Hard Routing Compute: 1 TFLOP (gating) + (1 * 20 TFLOPS) = 21 TFLOPS. Hard Routing VRAM: 0.5GB + (4 * 2GB) = 8.5GB (all models must be loaded to avoid PCIe transfer penalties). Compute saved = (81-21)/81 = ~74%, Memory saved = 0%.

title: Diagnosing Dynamic Ensemble Latency on A10G
scenario: A visual QA API uses a dynamic routing ensemble of 3 specialized Vision-Language Models (8GB FP16 each) and a 2GB gating model, deployed on AWS g5.xlarge instances (single A10G 24GB VRAM). The gating model selects one specialized model per request. During a period of highly mixed traffic, P99 latency spikes from 45ms to 545ms, while GPU compute utilization plummets to 20%.
common_mistake: Assuming the models are executing sequentially or that the GPU lacks compute power, suggesting an upgrade to an A100 instead of diagnosing the memory thrashing.
realistic_solution: The total ensemble size (26GB) exceeds the 24GB VRAM, causing PCIe swapping. Apply INT8 quantization to halve model sizes to 4GB, fitting all 14GB in VRAM, or deploy on multi-GPU instances with pinned models.
napkin_math: Total VRAM needed = (3 * 8GB) + 2GB = 26GB > 24GB VRAM limit. Swapping one 8GB model over PCIe Gen4 x16 (approx 16GB/s effective) takes ~500ms (8GB eviction + 8GB load). 45ms compute + 500ms swap = 545ms latency.

title: Calculating MFU for LLM Training
scenario: You are training a 20-billion parameter dense Transformer LLM on a cluster of 256 NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs. Each GPU has a theoretical peak of 312 TFLOPS in BF16. Your global batch size is 4 million tokens, and the training loop reports an average step time of 12.0 seconds. Assuming standard training (forward and backward pass), calculate the Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU).
common_mistake: Using 2*P (inference FLOPs) instead of 6*P (training FLOPs) per token, or including the overhead of activation checkpointing (which computes HFU, not MFU).
realistic_solution: Calculate the theoretical model FLOPs per step using the 6*P*D approximation, calculate the peak hardware FLOPs over the step time, and find the ratio.
napkin_math:
1. Theoretical FLOPs/step = 6 * Parameters * Tokens = 6 * 20e9 * 4e6 = 480e15 FLOPs (480 PFLOPs).
2. Hardware Peak FLOPs/sec = 256 GPUs * 312e12 FLOPs/sec = ~80e15 FLOPs/sec (80 PFLOPs/sec).
3. Observed Throughput = 480 PFLOPs / 12 seconds = 40 PFLOPs/sec.
4. MFU = Observed Throughput / Hardware Peak = 40 / 80 = 50%.

title: Evaluating Dynamic Ensembles for Fraud Detection
scenario: You are designing the serving architecture for a fraud detection system that experiences severe distribution shifts during flash sales and bot attacks. You have trained 3 specialized 13B parameter models (Normal, Flash-Sale, Bot-Attack), each requiring 26GB of memory in FP16. Your serving fleet uses A100 80GB GPUs. The baseline approach runs all 3 models in parallel for every request and averages the outputs. The business requires a P99 latency of <100ms at 10,000 QPS. Evaluate the trade-offs between maintaining the parallel ensemble, implementing a lightweight gating network for dynamic routi
common_mistake: Failing to calculate that 3x26GB = 78GB of weights on an 80GB GPU leaves only 2GB for KV cache and activations, crippling the maximum batch size and making the 10,000 QPS target unreachable without massively overprovisioning the fleet.
realistic_solution: Implement a lightweight gating network (e.g., 100M parameters) to route each request to a single specialized model. Deploy each 13B model on dedicated A100s, freeing 54GB per GPU for KV cache, allowing for large batch sizes and high throughput.
napkin_math: Parallel ensemble memory: 3 * 26GB = 78GB weights, leaving 2GB for batching. Routed ensemble: 26GB weights per GPU, leaving 54GB for batching. 54GB / 2GB = 27x increase in available memory for KV cache/activations, dramatically increasing max batch size and throughput while reducing inference compute by 66%.

title: Diagnosing Low MFU in LLM Training
scenario: You are profiling a 7B parameter LLM training job on an 8x NVIDIA A100-80GB node (peak 312 TFLOPS BF16 per GPU). The global batch size is 32, and sequence length is 2048. Your iteration time is 3.5 seconds per step. Your infrastructure monitoring shows 100% GPU utilization, but you suspect the actual mathematical efficiency is poor. Diagnose the root cause of this inefficiency, explicitly distinguishing between Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU) and Hardware FLOPS Utilization (HFU).
common_mistake: Relying on `nvidia-smi` utilization as a measure of compute efficiency, or failing to realize that activation checkpointing inflates HFU (Hardware FLOPS) without improving MFU (useful Model FLOPs).
realistic_solution: First, calculate actual MFU (~31.5%). Since `nvidia-smi` shows 100%, the GPUs are active but not performing dense math efficiently. The gap between HFU and MFU is often caused by activation recomputation. The overall low MFU is likely due to memory bandwidth bottlenecks from unfused element-wise operations, small micro-batch sizes leading to low arithmetic intensity, or blocking communication overheads.
napkin_math:
Tokens per step = 32 * 2048 = 65,536.
Model FLOPs per token ≈ 6 * 7B = 42 GFLOPs.
Total FLOPs/step = 65,536 * 42e9 ≈ 2.75e15 FLOPs (2752 TFLOPs).
Total throughput = 2752 TFLOPs / 3.5s = 786 TFLOPS.
Per-GPU throughput = 786 / 8 = 98.25 TFLOPS.
MFU = 98.25 / 312 (peak) ≈ 31.5%.

title: Diagnosing TensorRT Dynamic Shape Spikes
scenario: You are optimizing a PyTorch-based Transformer model for inference on NVIDIA A10G GPUs. You export the model to ONNX and compile it to a TensorRT engine using `trtexec` with dynamic axes enabled for sequence length. While P99 latency improves from 45ms to 12ms for a fixed sequence length of 128, production traffic with varying sequence lengths (32 to 512) causes the TensorRT engine to exhibit massive latency spikes up to 200ms and occasional OOM errors, whereas the original PyTorch model handled these variations predictably. Analyze the root cause of this severe performance degradation in the
common_mistake: Assuming TensorRT handles dynamic shapes natively at runtime exactly like PyTorch. Candidates often wrongly diagnose this as a batching configuration issue or a memory leak in the serving layer.
realistic_solution: TensorRT requires explicit Optimization Profiles (Min, Opt, Max shapes) at compile time to handle dynamic axes efficiently. If requests fall outside the specified profile or if profiles are missing, TensorRT triggers expensive runtime memory reallocations and falls back to unoptimized kernels. The fix is compiling the engine with explicit bounds (e.g., `--minShapes=input:1x32`, `--optShapes=input:1x128`, `--maxShapes=input:1x512`) to ensure proper ahead-of-time kernel selection and memory pre-allocation.
napkin_math: PyTorch at seq=512: ~150ms. TensorRT without max profile: triggers reallocation overhead (>100ms) + suboptimal kernel -> ~200ms. TensorRT with max=512 profile: statically allocates activation workspace for seq=512 at load time, runs optimal kernel -> ~40ms. Pre-allocation eliminates the 100ms+ allocation spikes and OOMs.

title: TensorRT Format Optimization Latency Calculation
scenario: You have a BERT-base PyTorch model deployed on an NVIDIA A10G GPU in AWS. Native PyTorch FP32 inference takes 18ms per request (batch size 1). You need to optimize the format using TensorRT with FP16 precision. Assume FP16 provides a 2x compute speedup due to Tensor Cores, and TensorRT layer fusion reduces memory bandwidth overhead by 30%. If the original 18ms latency was split into 10ms for compute and 8ms for memory overhead, calculate the expected inference latency after converting the model to an optimized TensorRT engine.
common_mistake: Applying a single flat speedup multiplier (like 2x for FP16) to the total latency, failing to separate compute-bound time from memory-bound time.
realistic_solution: Apply the speedup factors independently to the compute and memory portions of the latency before summing them for the final result.
napkin_math: Compute latency = 10ms / 2 = 5ms. Memory latency = 8ms * 0.70 = 5.6ms. Total expected latency = 5.0ms + 5.6ms = 10.6ms.

title: Black-Box Model Inversion Query Cost
scenario: You deploy a cloud-based medical pathology model (taking 256x256 grayscale images) behind a public API that returns exact float32 probability scores for 10 disease classes. The API charges $0.0001 per inference and rate-limits to 5,000 queries per minute. An attacker attempts a zero-th order (finite difference) black-box model inversion attack to reconstruct the prototypical image of a sensitive disease class. They use a standard 2-point finite difference estimation for the gradient of each pixel. Calculate the time and cost required to perform 100 optimization steps.
common_mistake: Assuming model inversion requires white-box weight access, or failing to multiply the input dimensionality (pixels) by the finite difference evaluations per pixel per step, drastically underestimating the attack's API cost.
realistic_solution: To estimate the gradient for a 256x256 image (65,536 pixels) using 2-point finite differences, each optimization step requires 131,072 queries. 100 steps require 13.1 million queries, costing $1,310.72 and taking ~43.7 hours at the rate limit. Defend by truncating output probabilities to low precision (e.g., 2 decimal places) or returning only the top-1 class, which neutralizes the attacker's numerical gradient signal.

title: Evaluating TensorRT vs ONNX for Dynamic Shapes
scenario: You are deploying a BERT-Large ranking model (340M parameters) on NVIDIA L4 GPUs for a real-time recommendation API. Traffic has highly dynamic sequence lengths (32 to 512 tokens) and batch sizes (1 to 32). The baseline PyTorch FP16 model yields a P99 latency of 45ms, failing the strict 20ms SLA. Evaluate the trade-offs between ONNX Runtime and TensorRT for this dynamic-shape workload, and justify an optimization strategy that minimizes latency jitter while maximizing throughput.
common_mistake: Blindly exporting to TensorRT with a single broad dynamic shape profile (min=1, opt=16, max=32), which forces the engine to select sub-optimal kernels or triggers runtime recompilation heuristics, leading to severe P99 latency jitter.
realistic_solution: Use TensorRT with discrete Optimization Profiles (e.g., seq_len buckets of 128, 256, 512) and pad incoming requests to the nearest bucket. This trades a predictable compute overhead from padding for highly optimized, static-like kernel execution.
napkin_math: PyTorch Baseline P99: 45ms. Broad dynamic TRT profile: 15ms base + 20ms kernel selection jitter = 35ms (fails SLA). With 3 discrete TRT profiles and padding, max padding compute overhead is ~33%. 12ms base compute * 1.33 padding factor = 16ms steady P99 latency (passes 20ms SLA).

title: Evaluating MFU for LLM Training on A100 Cluster
scenario: Your team is training a 70B parameter LLM on a cluster of 1024 NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPUs. The theoretical peak BF16 throughput of each A100 is 312 TFLOPS. Your current pipeline processes 400,000 tokens per second globally. You need to evaluate whether to invest 3 months of engineering effort into custom FlashAttention kernels and better communication overlap to increase Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU). How do you evaluate the current MFU to justify this decision?
common_mistake: Candidates often confuse Hardware FLOPS Utilization (HFU) with Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), incorrectly including the FLOPs from activation recomputation or padding in the numerator, which artificially inflates the utilization metric.
realistic_solution: Calculate the MFU using the theoretical minimum FLOPs per token (approx 6 * P for forward and backward passes). At 70B parameters and 400k tokens/sec, the useful throughput is 168 PFLOPS. Given the cluster peak of 319.5 PFLOPS, the MFU is 52.6%. Since highly optimized frameworks can achieve 60-65% MFU on A100s, the investment is justified: an absolute increase to 60% MFU would reduce training time by about 12%, saving millions in compute costs over a typical run.
napkin_math: Theoretical FLOPs/token ≈ 6 * P = 6 * 70e9 = 420 GFLOPs. Total achieved FLOPs/sec = 420 GFLOPs/token * 400,000 tokens/sec = 168 PFLOPS. Cluster peak = 1024 * 312 TFLOPS = 319.5 PFLOPS. MFU = 168 PFLOPS / 319.5 PFLOPS = 52.6%.

title: Cloud API Model Inversion Vulnerability
scenario: A cloud-hosted biometric authentication API serves a 50M parameter Vision Transformer on NVIDIA T4 GPUs at 1,000 QPS. The API returns the predicted identity along with an FP32 confidence score for the top-5 matches. A security audit reveals that an attacker is reconstructing recognizable faces of private users by querying the API roughly 50,000 times per target identity. Analyze the API design to identify the root cause enabling this reconstruction and propose a mitigation that does not require retraining the model.
common_mistake: Assuming the attack is membership inference, or believing that limiting to top-5 outputs (instead of the full softmax distribution) is sufficient to prevent gradient-based optimization.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the high-precision (FP32) confidence scores returned by the API, which provide a smooth continuous loss surface for the attacker to perform numerical gradient estimation. The solution is to truncate or round the confidence scores (e.g., to 1 decimal place or coarse intervals like 'High/Medium/Low') to create step functions that zero out the attacker's numerical gradients.
napkin_math: An attacker optimizing a 512-dimensional GAN latent space to reconstruct a face needs ~1,024 queries per gradient step via finite differences: (f(z+eps) - f(z)) / eps. For 50 steps, this is ~51,200 queries. If FP32 outputs are rounded to 2 decimal places (100 bins), f(z+eps) - f(z) evaluates to 0 for small eps, causing the gradient approximation to vanish and stalling the inversion attack.

title: Diagnosing PII Leakage in Fine-Tuned LLMs
scenario: Your team deployed a 70B parameter LLM fine-tuned on 500GB of proprietary customer support logs using a cluster of 64 H100 GPUs. During beta testing, a targeted prompt successfully extracted verbatim PII (names and SSNs). The training pipeline included aggressive exact-string deduplication and 3 epochs of training. As the ML systems lead, you need to analyze the data pipeline and training setup to determine why the exact-string deduplication failed to prevent this memorization.
common_mistake: Assuming exact deduplication prevents all repetition. Candidates miss that templated logs create near-duplicates, which bypass exact deduplication and drastically increase PII exposure across epochs.
realistic_solution: Implement fuzzy deduplication (e.g., MinHash LSH) to strip near-duplicates, strictly limit training on sensitive data to 1 epoch, and evaluate memorization risk using Membership Inference Attacks (MIA) pre-deployment.
napkin_math: 70B params = 140GB capacity. Training data = 500GB. If a PII string is in a template repeated 50 times across near-duplicate logs, 3 epochs = 150 exposures. Memorization of specific sequences typically occurs reliably after 10-100 exposures.

title: Mitigating PII Memorization in Clinical LLMs
scenario: You are training a 7B parameter clinical LLM from scratch on 500B tokens of raw medical records using a cluster of 256 H100 GPUs. The security and compliance teams mandate that the resulting model must not regurgitate patient PII (e.g., names, SSNs, exact medical histories) under targeted prompt injection attacks. You must evaluate three distinct mitigations: aggressive corpus deduplication/scrubbing, Differential Privacy training (DP-SGD), and post-training RLHF refusal guardrails. Justify your primary architectural choice quantitatively based on compute budget, utility degradation, and verif
common_mistake: Assuming post-training RLHF or system prompts can completely erase memorized PII, or proposing DP-SGD for base training without accounting for its massive memory/compute overhead and severe utility tax at the 7B parameter scale.
realistic_solution: Prioritize aggressive n-gram deduplication and strict PII scrubbing (NER/regex) on the pre-training corpus. Research shows eidetic memorization scales logarithmically with sequence repetition; deduplication prevents the frequency-based over-indexing that drives memorization. Avoid DP-SGD for base pre-training due to its high compute multiplier and utility drop. RLHF should only be applied as a secondary defense layer.
napkin_math: Standard 7B training on 500B tokens requires ~2.1e22 FLOPs (6 * 7e9 * 5e11). On 256 H100s (~1e15 FLOPS effective each), this takes ~2.4 days. DP-SGD requires per-sample gradient clipping; even with Ghost Clipping optimizations, the memory and compute overhead is roughly 3-4x, extending training to ~10 days and significantly degrading clinical perplexity to achieve an epsilon < 8. Conversely, distributed CPU-based corpus deduplication costs minimal compute time upfront and directly caps the sequence repetition count (k) required for extraction.

title: Diagnosing Inter-Node TP Bottlenecks
scenario: You are diagnosing a severe performance degradation during the pre-training of a 70B parameter dense transformer on two 8-GPU A100 (80GB) nodes connected via 400 Gbps InfiniBand. To fit the model and optimizer states, the team configured Megatron-style Tensor Parallelism (TP) to 16. Profiling reveals a Model Flop Utilization (MFU) of less than 15%, with GPUs spending the majority of their time stalled on synchronous NCCL All-Reduce kernels.
common_mistake: Attempting to hide the communication latency by increasing gradient accumulation steps or micro-batch sizes, not realizing that Tensor Parallelism requires synchronous communication within every transformer block's forward and backward pass.
realistic_solution: Restrict Tensor Parallelism to the intra-node NVLink domain (TP=8) to keep synchronous All-Reduces on high-bandwidth links. Distribute the rest of the model across the two nodes using Pipeline Parallelism (PP=2), which only sends activation boundaries over the slower inter-node link.
napkin_math: Intra-node NVLink provides ~600 GB/s of bandwidth, whereas 400 Gbps InfiniBand provides only ~50 GB/s (a 12x drop). A TP=16 configuration forces 2 massive All-Reduce operations per transformer layer to cross the 50 GB/s inter-node boundary, creating an insurmountable synchronous bottleneck.

title: Calculating Minimum Model Parallelism Degree
scenario: You are provisioning a training cluster for a 30-billion parameter transformer model using standard mixed-precision training (FP16 with Adam optimizer). Without using ZeRO-style optimizer state partitioning, the model states (weights, gradients, FP32 master weights, momentum, variance) must be distributed across devices using pure Model Parallelism. The cluster uses NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 40GB of HBM each. Assume you need to reserve 8GB per GPU strictly for activations and workspace. Calculate the minimum required degree of Model Parallelism to fit the training state.
common_mistake: Calculating memory requirements based only on the 16-bit model weights (which would be 60GB total, incorrectly implying just 2 GPUs), completely ignoring the 14 bytes/param overhead of gradients, master weights, and Adam optimizer states.
realistic_solution: In standard mixed-precision training, each parameter requires 16 bytes of memory (2 bytes for FP16 weights, 2 bytes for FP16 gradients, 4 bytes for FP32 master weights, 4 bytes for FP32 momentum, and 4 bytes for FP32 variance). A 30B parameter model thus requires 480GB of memory. With 32GB of usable memory per 40GB GPU, 15 GPUs are mathematically required. To maintain symmetrical division across attention heads and efficient communication topologies, the realistic minimum Model Parallelism degree is 16.
napkin_math:
State per param = 2 (FP16 W) + 2 (FP16 G) + 4 (FP32 W) + 4 (FP32 M) + 4 (FP32 V) = 16 bytes.
Total Model State = 30e9 * 16 bytes = 480 GB.
Usable GPU Memory = 40 GB - 8 GB (activations) = 32 GB.
Minimum GPUs = 480 GB / 32 GB = 15 GPUs.
Nearest hardware-friendly power of 2 = 16.

title: Mitigating Black-Box Model Inversion on Health APIs
scenario: You are deploying a 1.2B parameter Transformer model for clinical readmission risk scoring via a public REST API. The API currently returns full FP32 confidence vectors across 500 risk categories. Security audits show that an attacker can execute a black-box model inversion attack by making ~50,000 queries to reconstruct sensitive patient training data with 85% fidelity. You must evaluate and select a defense to protect the data while keeping API latency under 50ms and maintaining an absolute accuracy drop of less than 1.5%.
common_mistake: Jumping immediately to retraining the model with DP-SGD (Differential Privacy), which severely degrades model utility (often >5% accuracy drop) and requires massive GPU compute overhead, or relying solely on IP-based rate limiting which fails against distributed botnets.
realistic_solution: Restrict the API output payload. Instead of returning the full FP32 probability distribution, return only the Top-3 categories and round the confidence scores to one decimal place (e.g., 0.8 instead of 0.812345). This breaks the numerical gradient estimation required for optimization-based inversion attacks while adding zero latency.
napkin_math: Optimization-based inversion relies on estimating gradients ≈ [f(x+δ) - f(x)]/δ. If output is rounded to 0.1, a δ causing a true confidence shift of 0.04 yields 0 change in the API output. The attacker's gradient estimate becomes exactly 0. To bypass this, the attacker must randomly explore the space, increasing the required queries per step from O(d) to O(d / ε²). For d=500 and precision ε=0.1, the total API queries needed jumps from 5×10⁴ to >5×10⁷, effectively neutralizing the attack.

title: Evaluating TP and PP Placement for 175B LLMs
scenario: Your team is architecting the training cluster for a 175B parameter language model using FP16. The model states require over 2TB of memory, far exceeding a single A100 80GB GPU. You have a cluster of DGX A100 nodes (8 GPUs per node, 600 GB/s NVLink intra-node, 200 Gbps InfiniBand inter-node). You must evaluate and justify the optimal combination and placement of Tensor Parallelism (TP) and Pipeline Parallelism (PP) across this hardware topology to maximize MFU (Model Flops Utilization).
common_mistake: Placing Tensor Parallelism across node boundaries, failing to recognize that TP requires synchronous all-reduce operations on every layer, which will be severely bottlenecked by the slower inter-node InfiniBand.
realistic_solution: Restrict Tensor Parallelism strictly to intra-node GPUs (e.g., TP=8) to utilize the 600 GB/s NVLink for intensive all-reduce operations. Use Pipeline Parallelism (PP) across nodes, as PP only requires passing activation boundaries, which easily fits within the 25 GB/s InfiniBand capacity.
napkin_math: TP communication needs ~100GB/s per layer for typical LLM batches. NVLink provides 600GB/s (sufficient), but IB provides only 25GB/s (a 4x bottleneck). PP only transmits boundary activations (e.g., ~50GB per micro-batch), which can be efficiently pipelined over the 25GB/s IB link without stalling compute.

title: LLM Quantization Trade-offs on A100
scenario: You are evaluating the deployment architecture for a 70B parameter LLM for a chatbot service using A100 80GB GPUs (2 TB/s memory bandwidth). In FP16, the model weights consume ~140GB, requiring a 2-GPU tensor parallel setup. An alternative proposal is to apply INT4 weight-only quantization (e.g., AWQ or GPTQ) to reduce the weight footprint to 35GB, allowing the model and KV cache to fit on a single GPU. Evaluate the trade-offs between the FP16 multi-GPU and INT4 single-GPU deployments, justifying your decision mathematically based on decoding-phase throughput, hardware utilization, and cost.
common_mistake: Candidates often assume that INT4 quantization will slow down inference due to the computational overhead of de-quantizing weights back to FP16/FP32 for matrix multiplications, failing to realize that the decoding phase of LLMs is heavily memory-bandwidth bound.
realistic_solution: The optimal choice for throughput per dollar is the INT4 single-GPU setup. Because LLM generation is memory-bandwidth bound, reading fewer bytes from HBM increases generation speed. INT4 reduces the memory read per parameter by 4x, overcoming the de-quantization compute overhead, removing inter-GPU communication latency, and cutting hardware costs in half.
napkin_math: FP16 (2 GPUs): 140GB weights / (2 * 2000 GB/s HBM bandwidth) = 35 ms/token memory read time (assuming perfect scaling). INT4 (1 GPU): 35GB weights / (2000 GB/s HBM bandwidth) = 17.5 ms/token. INT4 yields ~2x the token generation speed on half the hardware.

title: GPU Sizing for 70B Model Sharding
scenario: You are deploying a 70-billion parameter LLM in FP16 precision for inference on a cluster featuring A10G GPUs (24GB VRAM each). Your target batch size and context length require an additional 20GB of total VRAM combined across the deployment for the KV cache and intermediate activations. Calculate the minimum number of GPUs required to shard this model using Tensor Parallelism, accounting for both memory capacity and optimal network communication topologies.
common_mistake: Candidates often only divide the weight memory by the GPU capacity (140GB / 24GB = 5.8 GPUs) and suggest 6 GPUs, forgetting to allocate space for the KV cache or ignoring that Tensor Parallelism highly favors power-of-2 GPU counts for efficient all-reduce operations.
realistic_solution: Calculate the total required memory footprint (160GB) and divide by the GPU capacity (24GB) to find the absolute capacity minimum (7 GPUs). Then, round up to the nearest power of 2 (8 GPUs) to ensure high-performance Tensor Parallelism communication and provide safety headroom.
napkin_math:
Weights: 70B params * 2 bytes/param (FP16) = 140 GB.
Total memory needed: 140 GB + 20 GB (KV cache & acts) = 160 GB.
Minimum GPUs by capacity: 160 GB / 24 GB/GPU = 6.66 GPUs (requires 7).
Optimal TP size (power of 2) -> 8 GPUs.
Total capacity: 8 * 24 = 192 GB (safe 32GB buffer).

title: LLM Inference Throughput with INT8 Quantization
scenario: You are deploying a 30 billion parameter autoregressive language model on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU (80GB HBM, 2039 GB/s memory bandwidth) for a low-latency chat application. The application operates with a batch size of 1. Assuming the generation phase is strictly memory-bandwidth bound and ignoring KV cache memory traffic for this calculation, calculate the theoretical maximum token generation rate (in tokens/second) when using FP16 weights versus INT8 weight-only quantization.
common_mistake: Candidates often assume that generation speed at batch size 1 is limited by GPU compute (TFLOPs) rather than memory bandwidth, failing to recognize that every weight must be loaded from HBM for each generated token.
